Claude.notes: Real-event story-scenes are accurate and tightly placed, but they function as short historical sidebars rather than character-driven narrative beats; lessons revert to concept-first exposition almost immediately, which keeps narrative density well below the Basic-level target of 50–65%.
Claude.topIssue: Story sections are short historical anecdotes rather than character-driven narrative; concept content dominates the lessons, leaving the protagonist (the learner) without a sustained arc to land insights inside.

Gemini.notes: Definitions of generative AI, RLHF, tokens, parameters, transformers, attention, and context windows are all technically correct and at appropriate depth. The lawyer-fake-citations case is correctly described. Some definitional precision is loose — the 'tokens are roughly ¾ of a word' framing understates how much tokenization varies across languages, code, and rare words.
Gemini.topIssue: Definitional precision could be tightened: 'roughly ¾ of a word per token' is a common shorthand but obscures that tokens range from a single character to a multi-word phrase depending on the tokenizer.

Perplexity.notes: By May 2026 the foundational concepts still hold, but the worked examples are dominated by 2022–2023 milestones (ChatGPT launch, GPT-3, GPT-4 Turbo, Attention Is All You Need). Newer reference points — GPT-5, Claude 4 family, current context-window scales, and the 2024-2025 evals — are absent.
Perplexity.topIssue: The lab JavaScript calls model `claude-opus-4-5`, which is not a published Claude model identifier (current production is the Opus 4.7 family). Real lab calls would 4xx and force the page into its fallback bank, which silently masks the bug.

Claude.notes: The Word2Vec opening, NY-lawyers-fake-citations beat, and prompt-engineering vignettes are well-chosen, but they read as illustrative side-quotes attached to expository sections rather than narrative throughlines. The 'craft' progression — vague to specific prompt — would land harder if a single learner-protagonist worked one prompt across all four lessons.
Claude.topIssue: Story-scenes feel like sidebars rather than problem-creating openings; the concept arrives independently of the narrative beat, so the learner is told what hallucination is rather than discovering it through the protagonist's failure.

Gemini.notes: The hallucination definition is solid and the bias source-attribution is correct. However, the Word2Vec attribution is wrong — Mikolov et al. published Word2Vec at Google in 2013, not 2016. The Mata v. Avianca lawyer case is correctly summarised in spirit but the firm name (Levidow, Levidow & Oberman) is presented without the case caption that would let learners verify it.
Gemini.topIssue: Word2Vec is attributed to a 2016 Google paper, but the original Word2Vec papers (Mikolov, Chen, Corrado, Dean) were published in 2013 — the date should be corrected.

Perplexity.notes: The CNET, AP, WGA, and Clarkesworld vignettes are all real but anchor the lesson firmly in 2022–2023. By May 2026, learners would expect to see the NYT v. OpenAI lawsuit (filed Dec 2023) and the post-WGA wave of newsroom AI policies that emerged in 2024-2025 (Reuters, Axel Springer, etc.).
Perplexity.topIssue: The CNET, WGA, and AP examples are 2022–2023; nothing from the 2024-2025 wave of newsroom AI policy or post-NYT-v-OpenAI fallout appears, leaving the 'how writers are using AI' lesson noticeably stale for 2026 learners.

Claude.notes: Strongest narrative-to-concept handoff in the first half of the course: Jason Allen → 'is it really art?' → diffusion concept; Sarah Andersen → 'who consented?' → training-data concept; Trump deepfake → 'can we still trust photos?' → visual-deception concept; Refik Anadol → 'AI as new medium' → AI-as-creative-tool concept. The story actively creates the question the concept answers.
Claude.topIssue: Story-to-concept handoff is the strongest in the course, but lessons still default to expository concept-first delivery once the anchor scene ends, so the narrative density still falls short of the Basic-level target.

Gemini.notes: Diffusion-model definition is correct, the latent-space metaphor is well-pitched for the level, and the LAION-5B figure (5.85B image-text pairs) matches the published number. The Andersen/McKernan/Ortiz class action is correctly summarised. The Trump-deepfake / Eliot Higgins / Bellingcat attribution is accurate.
Gemini.topIssue: Strong factual citations overall, but the LAION-5B section glides past the December 2023 Stanford disclosure that the dataset contained CSAM material — a material 2023-2024 development that belongs in any 2026 lesson on training-data ethics.

Perplexity.notes: The visual-AI landscape moved fast between 2023 and 2026. The lesson treats Midjourney, DALL·E, and Stable Diffusion as the canonical set, with no mention of Adobe Firefly's licensed-data model, OpenAI Sora (Feb 2024) for text-to-video, Google Veo, Black Forest Labs' FLUX, or the post-2024 image-generation tooling that learners actually encounter today.
Perplexity.topIssue: No mention of Sora, Veo, Adobe Firefly's licensed-data approach, or other 2024-2025 image/video developments that have meaningfully reshaped the visual-AI landscape since the cited 2022-2023 events.

Claude.notes: The four anchor stories — Daddy's Car (2016), MuseNet (2019), WGA strike (2023), Heart on My Sleeve (2023) — sequence beautifully from 'AI can imitate' to 'AI threatens working composers' to 'AI clones identity.' Each beat creates the question the concept section answers. Quiz question 'What's the hardest part of rhythm for AI?' edges into application territory.
Claude.topIssue: Each anchor story is treated as a standalone anecdote; the four-event arc would land harder if the lessons explicitly built on each other (e.g. opening L2 by referencing what Daddy's Car couldn't yet do).

Gemini.notes: Daddy's Car / Sony CSL / Flow Machines / Benoît Carré / 13,000 lead sheets — all correctly attributed. MuseNet trained on hundreds of thousands of MIDI files is approximately right. The Heart on My Sleeve / ghostwriter977 / April 2023 viral / UMG takedown story is correctly summarised. The Recording Academy ineligibility ruling is correctly recalled.
Gemini.topIssue: Factual attributions are clean, but the lesson misses an opportunity to explain that MIDI-based generation (MuseNet) is fundamentally different from waveform/audio generation (Suno, Udio) — a distinction that materially affects what AI music can sound like and why.

Perplexity.notes: Music-AI moved further between 2023 and 2026 than almost any creative field. Suno v3 / v4 (2024-2025), Udio, the RIAA's June 2024 lawsuits against Suno and Udio, the ELVIS Act in Tennessee (March 2024), and the No Fakes Act discussion are the dominant reference points for music-AI policy by mid-2026 — none of them appear here.
Perplexity.topIssue: No mention of Suno v3/v4, Udio, or the June 2024 RIAA lawsuits against Suno and Udio — the most consequential music-AI events of the 18 months preceding this lesson, and the central policy story a 2026 learner needs.

Claude.notes: Best-performing module in the course. The Greg Rutkowski opening literally creates the consent question; LAION-5B explains the mechanism; the Andersen/McKernan/Ortiz lawsuit shows the legal response; Zarya of the Dawn answers 'who owns the output?'; the EU AI Act closes with 'what rules are being written?' The four lessons read as a single argument unfolding rather than four parallel concept lectures.
Claude.topIssue: Strongest topic-narrative match in the course; consent/copyright cases directly create the questions the concept sections answer. The capability delta could still be sharpened — quiz items lean recall, while the lab debate prompts genuinely require judgment.

Gemini.notes: EU AI Act 2024 is correctly described as the world's first major risk-tiered AI law. LAION-5B figures match. The fair-use four-factor framing in Lab 2 ('Factor 1 — purpose of AI training') is legally accurate. Definitions of derivative work, public domain, and human authorship are at appropriate depth for the level.
Gemini.topIssue: The Andersen v. Stability AI case is presented as 'still ongoing as of 2024' but the October 2023 partial dismissal of several claims (Judge Orrick) and the August 2024 amended complaint should be reflected in any 2026 update.

Perplexity.notes: Conceptually the strongest module, and the cases (Rutkowski, Kashtanova, Andersen) hold up well. By May 2026 the conspicuous omission is NYT v. OpenAI (filed Dec 2023) — the largest US training-data copyright case currently in motion — and the 2024 wave of licensing deals (OpenAI–FT, OpenAI–News Corp, Reddit–Google, Anthropic–Reddit) that have reframed the consent debate.
Perplexity.topIssue: The lesson states the Andersen lawsuit 'is still ongoing as of 2024' — by May 2026 this needs an update reflecting the 2023 partial dismissal, the 2024 amended complaint, and the broader NYT v. OpenAI implications that are now central to the consent conversation.

Claude.notes: The Hollie Mengert opening is a strong, specific anchor for the 'voice gets extracted' problem. The labs ('Mapping Your Creative Signature', 'Drafting Your Creative AI Policy', 'Creative Voice Defense Plan') are the most action-oriented in the course — clearer capability delta. Weakness: cases LAION-5B / WGA strike / Zarya of the Dawn appear here for the second or third time and feel recycled rather than building.
Claude.topIssue: The Hollie Mengert opening is fresh, but the rest of the module recycles LAION-5B, the WGA strike, and Zarya of the Dawn from M5 — the narrative loses momentum because the concept sections don't introduce new evidence, only new framings.

Gemini.notes: Definitions of 'creative voice', 'creative dependency', 'directed use', and the LAION-5B re-explanation are accurate. The Hollie Mengert case is broadly correct, though some details about which platform hosted the offending model could be tightened (the 'Hollie Mengert AI' fine-tune existed primarily as a community Stable Diffusion LoRA before becoming associated with Invoke).
Gemini.topIssue: Hollie+ / Invoke AI specifics are slightly imprecise; the 'Hollie Mengert AI' model began as a community Stable Diffusion fine-tune and the platform-attribution chain in the lesson is loose.

Perplexity.notes: The 'protect your voice' framing is genuinely useful, but the practical defense advice is generic. By 2026, Glaze and Nightshade (University of Chicago, late 2023 / 2024) are the most-discussed actual defensive tools available to living artists, and a working AI-literacy course should at least name them. Have I Been Trained is mentioned in M5; it should resurface here as a verification step.
Perplexity.topIssue: Defense plan advice doesn't address Glaze or Nightshade (UChicago, 2023-2024), which are the most-discussed practical defenses for living artists by 2026 and the obvious tooling answer to the 'protect your voice' framing.

modules.module: M7: AI as a Creative Partner

Claude.notes: The Sunspring 2016 / Oscar Sharp / Benjamin opening is the freshest case in the course and well-positioned for the 'creative partner' theme. The 'four elements of a strong prompt' framework in L2 is the clearest applied scaffold the course offers. But the same Jason Allen / Heart on My Sleeve / Zarya of the Dawn cases reappear here from M3, M4, and M5 — by Module 7 the case bench feels thin.
Claude.topIssue: Sunspring is a great fresh hook but the same Jason Allen, Heart on My Sleeve, and Zarya of the Dawn cases reappear from earlier modules, weakening the narrative as a fresh chapter.

Gemini.notes: Sunspring (2016, dir. Oscar Sharp, AI named Benjamin) is correctly attributed; Christie's Edmond de Belamy sale (~$432,500 in 2018) is correctly placed; Robin Sloan's custom language model writing experiment is correctly characterized. The 'four elements of a creative prompt' framework is well-grounded in published prompt-engineering practice.
Gemini.topIssue: Strong factual fidelity, but the L3 quiz item about Robin Sloan's custom model could be sharper — the model he actually used was a small RNN trained on his own corpus, not a public LLM, and that distinction is what makes the example interesting.

Perplexity.notes: The cited cases — Sunspring (2016), Christie's Edmond de Belamy (2018), AIVA, Jason Allen (2022) — are 6 to 10 years old. By 2026 the 'human-AI creative partnership' conversation centers on agentic workflow tools (Cursor, Claude Code, ChatGPT Canvas, Notion AI), text-to-video Sora/Veo workflows, and the Adobe Firefly/Photoshop generative-fill loop — none of which appear.
Perplexity.topIssue: AIVA, Sunspring, and the Christie's Edmond de Belamy auction are 2016-2018 events; the lesson is missing the 2024-2025 generative-music tooling and the agentic-AI creative workflow shift that defines 'AI as creative partner' for current learners.

modules.module: M8: The Future of Creative Work

Claude.notes: The Lab 4 '5-Year Creative Plan' is the strongest applied-outcome lab in the course — clearest capability delta of any lesson. But narrative integrity drops because the WGA strike, Zarya of the Dawn, and Heart on My Sleeve all return for the third or fourth time. By M8 these cases have stopped doing narrative work — they're being cited rather than told.
Claude.topIssue: Heaviest case-reuse in the course (WGA, Zarya, Heart on My Sleeve all return for the third or fourth time) — narrative momentum stalls and the 'future' framing isn't anchored in genuinely new evidence.

Gemini.notes: Some claims here are loosely sourced. The Adobe / College Board / AP Art and Design partnership claim and the '92% expect to use AI' figure are presented without enough specificity for a learner to verify them. The McKinsey 2023 framing is broadly defensible but the specific claim about 'creative directors who can direct AI tools' as the top growth role is not a verbatim McKinsey finding.
Gemini.topIssue: The Adobe State of Creativity / College Board / AP Art and Design integration claim is loosely attributed; specific percentages and partnership scope are presented as fact but require a citation chain that isn't provided.

Perplexity.notes: The 'future of creative work' module is the most time-sensitive in the course and the most dated. By May 2026, the post-2024 SAG-AFTRA settlement, the music-AI lawsuits (RIAA v. Suno/Udio, June 2024), the NYT v. OpenAI litigation, the wave of newsroom AI policies, and the Adobe Firefly Web/Photoshop generative-fill normalization should anchor the labor and tools discussion — instead the entire module is anchored in 2023.
Perplexity.topIssue: 'Future of creative work' content is anchored entirely in 2023-2024 data; by May 2026 the labor landscape has visibly shifted (post-2024 SAG-AFTRA, RIAA-v-Suno/Udio, NYT-v-OpenAI, agentic-AI workflows) and none of it is reflected.
